title: Insufficient DNS Monitoring
description: Learn how attackers abuse DNS traffic to secretly communicate with compromised systems and transfer data without being detected.

attack_stages.title: Normal DNS Traffic
attack_stages.description: Devices use DNS to translate website names into IP addresses before communicating over the network.

attack_stages.title: Insufficient DNS Monitoring
attack_stages.description: The organization does not closely inspect or monitor DNS traffic for unusual behavior.

attack_stages.title: DNS Tunneling
attack_stages.description: The attacker disguises malicious communication as normal DNS requests.

attack_stages.title: Hidden Communication
attack_stages.description: The compromised device secretly exchanges information with the attacker's server.

attack_stages.title: Data Exfiltration
attack_stages.description: Sensitive information leaves the organization's network without being detected.

prevention_points.title: Monitor DNS Traffic
prevention_points.description: Continuously inspect DNS queries for unusual or suspicious behavior.

prevention_points.title: DNS Query Whitelisting
prevention_points.description: Allow DNS requests only to trusted domains whenever possible.

prevention_points.title: DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC)
prevention_points.description: Use DNSSEC to improve the authenticity and integrity of DNS communications.

prevention_points.title: DNS Monitoring Tools
prevention_points.description: Use DNS monitoring solutions to detect tunneling attempts and abnormal DNS activity.

nodes.title: Scenario
nodes.text: An organization relies on DNS servers so employees can access websites, cloud services, and internal applications throughout the day.

nodes.title: What is DNS?
nodes.text: The Domain Name System (DNS) converts website names such as example.com into IP addresses that computers use to communicate. Almost every network request begins with a DNS lookup.

nodes.title: Insufficient DNS Monitoring
nodes.text: Because DNS traffic is often trusted, organizations may not inspect it carefully. Attackers take advantage of this by hiding malicious communication inside what appears to be normal DNS traffic.

nodes.title: Attacker Goal
nodes.text: The attacker wants to secretly communicate with a compromised computer and transfer information without triggering security alerts.

nodes.title: How can an attacker hide malicious communication on the network?

choices.text: Disguise malicious traffic as normal DNS requests (DNS tunneling).
Disconnect the DNS server from the network.

nodes.title: Try Again
nodes.text: DNS tunneling works by hiding malicious communication inside normal-looking DNS requests, making it difficult to detect if DNS traffic is not monitored.

nodes.title: DNS Tunneling
nodes.text: The attacker disguises malicious communication as ordinary DNS queries. Because the organization does not closely inspect DNS traffic, these communications pass through the network unnoticed.

nodes.title: Impact
nodes.text: The attacker secretly transfers sensitive information from the organization's network to an external server. Malware may also receive commands through the same hidden communication channel.

nodes.title: How to Prevent This
nodes.text: Organizations should continuously monitor DNS traffic, limit unnecessary DNS requests, implement DNS query whitelists, deploy DNSSEC where appropriate, and use DNS monitoring tools to detect tunneling attempts and suspicious activity.

nodes.title: Attack Replay
nodes.text: Review how an attacker hid malicious communication inside normal DNS traffic to secretly communicate with a compromised device and extract sensitive information.

nodes.title: Scenario Complete
nodes.text: DNS is essential for network communication, but weak DNS monitoring allows attackers to abuse it for covert communication and data theft. Monitoring DNS activity is an important layer of network defense.
